revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:初穂]]


{{Multiple issues|unreferenced=2017-08-28|original research=2017-08-28}}
'''Hatsuho''' (初穂) refers to the first ears of rice harvested in Japan, which are offered to the [[kami]] (deities) prior to the autumn rice harvest. It is also written as '''早穂''', '''荷前''', or '''最華''', all pronounced "hatsuho."<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=初穂|encyclopedia=World Encyclopedia|accessdate=2021-11-16}}</ref>

== Overview ==
In [[:en:Ancient Japan|Ancient Japan]], hatsuho referred to offerings collected from subjects by the ruling clans (gōzoku) to fund rituals and provide offerings to the deities. Later, as the authority of the clans was supplanted by the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} and the [[ritsuryō government]] was established, hatsuho evolved into a component of the [[rice tax]] (田租, "so") paid to local [[provincial offices]] (kokufu) representing the central government. This eventually became one of the three parts of the [[so-yō-chō system]].

Today, at the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], a ritual known as the *Nukihosai* is conducted before the [[Kanname-sai]] (*Shinjōsai*). Other {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} and some regions also perform early rice harvesting ceremonies (*nukibo*) before the harvest festivals (*niiname-sai*) during occasions like [[Hassaku]] or [[Chōyō Festival]]. This practice is believed to reflect the original form of hatsuho.<ref name=Saito>{{Cite book|author=Michiko Saito|title=Shinto Encyclopedia|edition=Condensed Edition|chapter=Hatsuho|editor=Institute for Japanese Culture Studies, Kokugakuin University|publisher=Kōbundō|year=1999|page=206}}</ref>

Initially, hatsuho referred strictly to rice or other [[:en:grains|grains]], but the term later expanded to include the first fruits, vegetables, seafood, or game of the year offered to kami or [[:en:Buddhas|Buddhas]].<ref name=Saito/> Furthermore, monetary offerings made in place of these first harvests also came to be called hatsuho.<ref name=Saito/> Today, monetary offerings at shrines are often referred to as "hatsuho-ryō," a term derived from this practice. Even when offering rice, the original rice ears are replaced with scattered grains (*sanmai*) or wrapped in white paper as offerings known as *ohineri*. In some cases, cooked rice or rice cakes are used as offerings.<ref name=Saito/>

This custom of offering the year's first harvest to the deities also evolved into the practice of appreciating the season's "first produce" (*hatsumono*). People began to exchange these early products as gifts among neighbors or acquaintances, further extending the cultural significance of hatsuho.<ref name=Saito/>
